CURRENT AFFAIRS,

At the last half-yearly meeting of the Great Sourface
Railway, Mr. M. Sullivan proposed—* That as the divi-
dends of the Ordinary shareholders have seriously de-
creased during recent years, the directors’ fees should
Dbe reduced, and they are hereby reduced from £2,500
to £2,000 each half-year.” The shareholders were get-
ting, he said, £72,000 less than they were getting a
few years ago; people who were getting £40 a year a
few years ago, were now ouly receiving £29. He com-
plained that though nominally there were 12 directors,
there were only three or four running the concern. The
directors were elected by co-option, and the shareholders
were then asked as a matber of form to re-elect the co-
opted “divectors. Mr. Sullivan, in the course of his
remarks, as reported, said:—“The day has come when
thie shareholders ought to form a Committee to meet the

directors and inquire why it was that their dividends
had seriously come down, and what could ‘be done to
restore them (applause).”

We agree with Mr. Sullivan, and we hope the com-
mittee of shareholders will be formed for the purpose
suggested. The plan of co-oplion and proxies places the
railway in the hands of a clique. When a vacancy occurs
on the Board, Brother Goulding and Co. co-opt a chum
and then before the meetings come round, “by order
Francis B. Ormsby, Secretary,” requests for proxies
are sent out, and owing to the natural imertia of
shareholders in big companies, the proxies are sent in
to the powers that be and the places of the co-opted
chums are ratified. The proxies sent in to Mr. Sulli-
van and his friends the last time, however, were very
formidable, and no doubt gave Brother Goulding a
spasm. The Inner Guard Sword-beaver of the Free-
masons of Ireland was peppery concerning Mr. Sulli-
van's resolution and speech. Brother Goulding does
not love Mr. Sullivan. According to the Freeman re-
port, he said :—“With regard to Mr. Sullivan’s amend-
ment he would correct his figures. Apparently Mr.
Sullivan had forgotten -that since they reduced the
Board they gave £400 of the fees to the fire insurance
fund of the company. He did not propose to discuss
them, or to follow Mr. Sullivan in his remarks on the
directors and their remuneration.  They could get a
chairman, directors and managers at any price they
liked, and of various degrees of ability and business
capacity. If a resolution like Mr. Sullivan's was
carried, the proprietors would have the additional
trouble of appointing another Board of the business
capacity of Mr. Sullivan and his friends, to work that
great undertaking.”  Brother Goulding was hoighty-
toighty. After all it was a good sign, a sign that the
work of the committee with which Mr. Sullivan is so
intimately connected is having a keen effect on the
brazen Brother. Well, it would be no calamity if
Brother Goulding and his co-opted chums resigned their
jobs; but Brother Goulding and the chums have no
notion that way. If they are to go out, the share-
holders must kick them out. We trust that the com-
mittee suggested by Mr. Sullivan will be formed with-
out delay. The shareholders’ property is a valuable one,
and they would be fools to allow it unreservedly to the
management of the Board that is a result of the
unsatisfactory system of co-option and proxy gathering.
How many shares do the highly-paid directors hold
between them?

The particulars of the meeting of the Provisional Com-
mittee of the proposed Dublin Industrial Development
Association arc not altogether satisfactory. The fact
that Alderman Cole, who believes in customers giving a
grant-in-aid to needy manufacturers, was in the chair,
was, in itself, enough to cause the head of any sensible
man to shake at the prospects of the proposed Associa-
tion. Now, we understand that the sense of the Confer-
ence was decidedly in favour of the “as good and as

18

<heap ” principle, so that it is strange that an Economic
Don Quixote should have been placed in the chair at the
meeting of the Provisional Committee. Those present
were not very representative of industry or commerce,
and if the manufacturers are not well to the fore in this
Association, we predict that it will not march. We were
not surprised to see that Mr. Jack-in-the-Box, M.P., was
joresent.

A sample of a shamrock box, which is being sold in
Cork, has been sentto us. One side of the box contains
ihe following lines : —

“T send you these Shamrocks, Mavourneen,
From your own old native home,

To remind you of Cork on Patrick’s Day,

With my blessing wherever you roam.”

‘It might be as well to inform those who intend sending
-shamrock to “ Mavourneen ” that these boxes are made
in England. What does the Cork Industrial Association
think of this attempt to have shamrock packed in Saxon
boxes? Excellent shamrock boxes retailed at 1d., 2d.,
and 3d., are made by the firm of P. O'Reilly, of Poolbeg
Street, Dublin, the eminent box manufacturers. These
Irish shamrock boxes ought to knock imported boxes
clean out of the market, and we hope our Cork readers,
who may be sending shamrock abroad, will insist on
seeing these boxes before they pay their money for im-
ported boxes adorned with a green rhyme and green
shamrocks.  Stationers and florists throughout the
country should have a stock of these Irish boxes on hand
in anticipation of the shamrock exporting traffic.

The annual meeting of the Cork Branch of the so-
cailed ““ National Amalgamated Union of Shop Assis-
tants, Warehousemen, and Clerks ”—a British institu-
tioa like the British Institute of Journalists—was held
ently. Our readers will remember that there is a
net Irish Drapers Assistants’ Organisation, and this
British Organisation, like Strongbow, has got a footing
on Trish ground, and is attempting, but, we understand,
with no great success, to increase its hold on Ireland.
The wily authorities of the:Cork Branch of this British
Association made a bid for popularity by inviting a de-
putation from the Cork Industrial Development Associa-
tion to its annual meeting. A deputation from the latter
body attended, and we think they did quite right in
doing so. One of the deputation—Mr. W. Roche—ac-
cording to the report in Croshie’s “only National,”
said :—“ It was not to the interest of the Irish shop as-
sistants, or to the interest of the country generally, to
try and palm off the goods of other countries as Irish
goods (hear, hear).” Might we suggest.that it is not in
the interests of the country that these particular Cork
shop assistants should try to palm off a British Associa-
tion on their fellow-workers. We want Home Rule;
and surely, if we are able to legislate for ourselves, we
ought to be able to keep our own Shop Assistants’ Asso-
ciation without, tying it on as an appendage to a British
TInstitution. This semi-British body talks of supporting
Irish Industry. Why does it not support the distinct
Irish Organisation

At Kingscourt, in the County of Cavan, the local
«dramatic club recently produced “The Moment of Peril.”
‘We never heard of that play, but the name suggests
‘Queen’s Theatre melodrama. Now we are informed
that Kingscourt is “the most Anglicised, beggarly town
if its size in Treland.” We hear that a branch of the
Gaelic League was established in the town about a year
ago, but that it never got so far as the formation of a

+ class, and that it is now dead.

_ John Redmond, during the recent debate in the Tmpe-
rial Parliament, said—" You might as well tell the whole

grubh ab once ;" and at the heel of the hunt two startling
{I;(l,lbbel:‘s were read. Now it would have been much better
and have avoided a lot of unpleasantness if the dear offi-
cial organ of the Gacelic Leagué had overcome its 1:hs{l)xlke
to this paper, and answered our note of J. f\-nugrgbt Lz 2 .c,fL,
asking when we might expect the Report an ) siomeny
of Accounts. The official organ tried to ignore our fair
and proper question, and subsequently when b?;e suiua—
tion developed—for it is harder to ignore our V‘Ll o selves
than some hoighty-toighty people may think—the  con-
frere” of the official organ screeched at us as enemies ot
the Irish Nation, and stated in effect that every shilling
given to the Language Collection would be a-blow at the
Leaper.  Of course, level-headed readers of the L'E}ADEM
will not be influenced in the slightest in their attitude
towards the Language Fund by S“Ck an ztbsu}"d screech
on the part of the official organ‘s ‘‘confrere.” But it
were time that the President of the League, or someone
else of influence, gave a talking to those ab hfzadqu:ar('/efs
with a view to the avoiding, or trying to avoid, seriously
compromising the organisation by their bl\mdc‘rmg and
want of tact. Again we had a member of the Executive
Committee—a Mr. Henry Morris—making an exhibition
of himself in some anonymous notes in the Dundalk
Democrat by shouting “Judge Keogh” ab us! Thesesort of
silly exhibitions on the part of a member of the Execu-
tive, do no harm to us, but they are calculated to do con-
siderable harm to the Gaelic League Organisation.
O’Connell, referring, at one of his meeting, to someone
who was interrupting him, asked— Is there anyone who
will put a wisp of hay in that fellow’s mouth?” Those
who have the best interests of the Gaelic League Organi-
sation at heart could find some use for a few wisps of hay
also.

At last the official organ has to announce that “the
Aunual Report and Financial Statements for 1903-4, as
submitted to the last Qpo-fenp are, as usual, being
issued to the public.” The official organ might as well
have answered our fair and proper question asked in our
issue of January 21st, for the public will now probably
thank the LEeapEr alone for the publishing to the public
of the Report and Financial Statements for 1903-4, pre-
sented to the &po-ferp last August—about seven
months ago. We have not yet been officially supplied
with the Report and Financial Statements, and we do
not need them now; for, as our readers know, a kind
friend some weeks ago supplied us with a copy, and in
face of the hoighty-toighty attitude of the official organ
we had no hesitation in discussing the accounts in our
columns in our issue of February 18th.

But the Gaelic League has done more : it has issued a
printed slip containing a brief account of its income and
expenditure for the eleven months ending the 31st
January of this year, which is signed by its treasurer.
We have already seen that during the year ending 31st
March, 1903, the official organ and self-styled « confrere”
of the King’s Most Excellent Majesty, the Lords and
Commons of Ireland organ, drew on the Language Fund
to the extent of £280; during the eleven months end-
ing February 29th, 1904, it drew the enormous sum of
£611 7s., and now we find from the statement of re-
ceipts and expenditure for the eleven months ending
January 31st last that it drew a further £200 out of the
Language till ; in all, the accounts covering a period of
two years and ten months, show that it drew the vast
sum of £1,091 7s. (one thousand and ninety-one pounds,
soven shillings). Tt certainly was a dear enterprise for
the Gaelic League. Itis added in the latest statement
that the last subsidy out of the funds of the League
(:£200) was made in April of last year to meet liabilities
that occurred during the previous financial year, and that
since that date (we presume the end of the last financial
year) the official organ is self-supporting. According to
this it would seem that the official organ’ should have
diawn £811 7s. instead of £611 7s. during the eleven
months ending February 29th, 1904, in order to steady
itself. Is the official organ self-supporting now? of
course we accept the assurance that it is in all good
faith ; ‘but we would like to see a statement of if: ac-
counts. Even the proprietor of a concern does not know

‘How he stands with regard to the year's working until
his books are made up ; and as'it is not claimed that the
official organ is earning anything, but only that it is
Self'”suppol‘bulg, we do not think anyone can be quite
satisfied on the point of fact until the accounts are mado
up. The monthly Gaclic Journal has received a subsidy
during the‘eleven months of £150, that is at the rate of
about £13 13s. per issue.

One cannot learn anything satisfactory, of cowrse, con-
cerning the position of the book department from a mere
statement of income and expenditure. We see nothing
charged against the book department under the head of
rent, though it occupies a valuable ground-floor shop
with frontage to O’Connell Street; but there is an item
of £33 1Is. 5d. under the head of ‘“ General Expenses,”
whatever they are. The salavies are £356 8., which
work out at nearly £7 a week. The recently-appointed
non-Trish-speaking traveller does not add much to this
item, as he is not long appointed, so that the salary list
appears to us as rather high. Altogether, though we see
no item for rent the expenditure on the book clci)nrl.mcnt
was £2,159 19s. 6d., and the book sale receipts
£2,249 6s. 10d. ; that is an excess of receipts over ex-
penditure of £79 7s. 4d. in the eleven months’ working
ending January 31st last.  Of course, this does not 1)1'0\':-
very much; one cannot say from these figures whether
.ﬂ;helbook department was worked at a loss or at a profit
during the eleven months’ working, tha depends upon
the value of the stock brought forward and the value of
the stock carried forward. ¥

The subsidy that had to be given to the Oireachtas,
notwithstanding that there is a special Oireachtas Fund
and that there is a considerable amount of vat&moucy:
was £410. That strikes us as being ratherbhigh, and,
perhaps, more efficient management of that great festival
might result in reducing the drain on the Qccnt‘l‘lll funds
f\f the League on that head ; however, we would be sorry
to advocate any cheese-paring on the Oireachtas ; it is a
festival that should, at any reasonable cost, be dono
well, and if it must be a charge on the funds to the cx-
tent of £410, we take no exception so long as the occa-
sion is done justice to. z

The amount paid to organisers for salaries and ex-
penses was only £1,245 5s., so that the amount of sub-
sidy given to the official organ, as shown by the accounts
for a period of two years and ten months—namel )
£1,091, would have almost kept the whole staff of orga-
nisers going for eleven months =

As our readers know, we sometimes see a copy of, and
sometimes refer to the Church of England’s Faithful
'G’arrz.wn in Ireland Gazetle, which comically misnames
itself “Z'he Church of Ireland” Gazelte. Some time ago
we heard there was a question whether it libelled the
President of the Gaclic League or not in some thing or
things that appeared in its columns ; we did not sce the
copy or copies containing the matter complained of, and
can express no opinion upon the subject beyond stating
the fact that there was some rumpus over the matter.
The last time that we noticed the Clurch of Eingland's
Faithful Garrison in Ireland Guazette was, we think, in
our issue of December 17th, 1904. In an article that ap-
peared in it upon which we commented, it said :—“ We
do not know if many of our readers are in the habit of
turning their attention to the pages of the Leaper, that
journalistic product of our metropolis, which is the clever,
but vulgar and unscrupulous, organ of the Roman Priest-
hood in Treland.” We added by way of comment : —“We
thought ourselves that we were the organ of Irish Ire-
land, but our ‘saved’ and scholarly contemporary knows
better! If it said that we are the organ of the ‘ surpliced
ruffians’ it might be intolerant, and raking up sectarian
animosity on our part to correct its error; if our contem-
porary said that we were the organ of Dr. Long or of
* Mike ’ it might be bad manners on our part to contra-
dict “our betters.’” We treated our “saved ” contem-
porary to some further comment, and made a further
quotation from its article. Well, we see in the official

notes in our “saved” contemporary, in which it was
thought recently there were references that might be
libellous on the President of the Gaelic League. T]'"Ae
official organ of the non-sectarian Gaelic League says in
the first sentonce of its notes:—* The Church of Ireland
Gazette is an opponent which we respect, profou‘nc'uy &
wo differ from it in most, of our aspirations and opinions.

Our hoighty-toighty contemporary that, according to ac-
counts covering two years and ten months, cost the Lan-
guage Fund the tidy little sum of £1,091, has respect: fm;
our “saved ” contemporary ! There you are; the “saved”

in this country, as we so often point out, are the re-
cipients of far too much ostentatious respect. Now the
article in the Church of England’s Iaithful Garrison
in Ireland Gazette, for which paper the non-sectarian offi-
cial organ of the Gaelic League goes out of its way to ex-
press respect, is entitled “The Irish Language and Na-
tionality,” and in the course of it our “saved” contempor-
ary has a “wipe” at the nefarious LEADER, which, in one
of its last December issues, it calls “the clever, but vulgar
and' unscrupulous, organ of the Roman Priesthood in
Ireland.” Our “saved” contemporary, for which the
official organ of the Gaelic League has respect, is prob-
ably yet smarting under the notes that we treated it to
last December, and under other wounds, for in the
article in its issue of February 10th, which the official
organ  discusses, we see a reference to our
“vulgar and unscrupulous”  selves; in  that
article, it is stated that we support “in
coarse and brutal language a campaign of religious
terrorism.” That is the way that the Church of Eng-
land’s Faithful Garrison in Ireland Gazette refers to our
almosti single-handed fight in the Press for Tolera-
tion, Justice, and Fair Play for citizens of Ireland who
have the Economic misfortune to be “ Idolator No
doubt when the editor of the official organ of the Gaelic
League, that cost the Language Fund in recent years a
sum of £1,091, saw the reference to cur supporting “in
coarse and brutal language a campaign of religious ter-
rorism,” his heart throbbed with joy. He would probably
welcome any sort of a mud flung ab us, as he is not able
to get in a blow at us himself. = Anyway, the official
organ of the Gaelic League, that is so hoighty-toighty
that it turns up its mighty nose at the organ of Irish Ire-
land, goes out of the way to express its respect for our
“saved ” contemporary, that in the very article under
discussion stated that the LEaDER supports “in coarse
and brutal language a campaign of religious terrorism.”

The claim recently made by a correspondent, Connac
Mupcana, that Leinster was  the tax-paying province,”
the “tribute-paying province” in relation to the Lan-
guage Fund, looks rather comical in view of the pub-
lished analysis of the Fund. Apart from subscriptions
sent, direct to (he Treasurer of the League, the contribu-~
tions from the four provinces work out as follows : —

SR eI
Leinster 846 19 1
Munster 704 18 7
Ulster 281 13 6
Connacht 173 18 0

Now, of the £846 19s 1d. credited to Leinster, Dublin
County subscribed £655 5s. 8d., leaving a balance for
the rest of Leinster of only £191 13s. 5d. Dublin being
the metropolis it has, of course, a great deal more peopler
from the other provinces residing in it than any other
city, and it is fair to assume that a considerable amount
of the £655 bs. 8d. was subscribed by natives of Muuster,
Ulster, and Connacht. Leinster, with the advantage of
Dublin,only contributed £142 0s. 6d. more than Muuster,
so that, if allowance was made for the subscriptions of
non-nabives in each province, it is probable that at least
Munster subscribed quite as much as Leinster. Con-
nacht, with its £173 18s., did not send in enough to pay
the last subsidy to the official organ.

In a letter from An cataip Catsoi ta Dpeanvdm i
reply to one from Conn Mac Mupcada published in the
official organ, the matter of provincial contributions is
incidentally veferred to. ~In the course of the letter
An tdtaip Cataoip pertinently says:—“If we wish to
discover which is really the tribute-paying province, that

i any province may lay clam to the title beyond an-
er,  we must estimate the swms expended in local
organisation, in the up-keep of branches, in the working
of Feiseanna, as well as the contributions to the
Sewhﬁmh_a.in na Gaedhilge collection. I know that the
gross receipts of Craobh na nAirne for one year, including
the receipts of its Feis, totalled over £500. This was
a handsome contribution to the cause of Irish Ireland
f‘m_m a rather impoverished district. Suppose now we
estimate the sums represented by the Tralee Feis, by the
Dingle Feis, which lasted thrée days, by the Iveragh
Feis, the Kenmare Feis, and the Killorglin Feis, each of
which was a two-day event. Let us add to them the
other receipts of these branches through the year, and
the receipts of the various other branches which held no
Feiseanna. Finally let us include the subscriptions sent,
by Kerry to the Language Fund, and then we may have
a fairly accurate idea of the tribute this county has paid.
A similar process applied to the other counties would
give us the result for the province. Let us do the same
in the remaining provinces and compare results. I fear
if such an estimate were made your correspondent would
be forced to withdraw the claim he makes with such as-
surance for Leinster.”

is,

One of the strong points—a permanently strong point

—is that only what we might call a small amount of cen-
tralisation is necessary and desirable'in the Gaelic Move-
ment. Evidently Wexford did not send much to the
Central Fund though it is generally acknowledged that
the Language Movement has made wonderful progress in
that, until recently, purely English-speaking county.
That county must spend a considerable sum of money
on the movement in Wexford, and, no doubt, those con-
cerned are convinced that they can work the movement
locally off their own bats. The Central Language Fund
is not any measure of the extent or growth of the move-
ment all over the country. In fact the progress of the
movement is, to a great extent, registered by the amount
of responsibilities that the Central Organisation can
delegate to local effort and initiative, and to private
enterprise. If, for instance, it was shown that owing to
_the enterprise of publishing firms and individual authors
that the work of keeping up an official publishing depart-
ment ceased to be necessary, and became undesirable, the
shedding of that department by the Central Organisation
would not be a sign of weakness, but a sign of growth.
If, again, a county wished to have the services of an orga-
niser all to itself, and subject to its directions, the fact
that it took over the charge of an organiser, and that one
organiser less was, in consequence, needed by the Central
Organisation, that would not indicate retrogression, but
rather progress and the development of keen local in-
terest in the Language Cause.

The chief expenditurc of the League is on the head
of organisers and head office salaries. Now a
development in the way of counties, or collections of
counties, undertaking the responsibility, financial and
otherwise, of their own organisers would lighten the
financial responsibilities of the League Organisation as a
whole, and add local knowledge and supervision to the
directing of the work of the organiser. A development
in that direction would help to relieve the Organisation,
as a whole, of a big financidl responsibility. ~ Father
Brennan, who is a member of the Executive, and whose
great practical work for the Language and the movement
renders his opinions of great value, says, in the course. of
the letter from which we have already quoted :—* The
selection of an organiser for Leinster will make the fourth
new salaried appointment made by the Coisde Gnotha
within the last four months. This may explain the  ap-
parent, shilly-shallying’ which has been a source of such
keen disappointment to the comprehensive observer
‘ who regardsthe development of the League Organisation
as a whole.” Besides there must be a certain amount of
opposition to the appointing of new organisers until some.
honest, attempt has first been made to solve the question

"of methods. At present the League seems to have no

definite policy as to the disposal and direction of its
organisers.”

Now if—according to one who is on the Executive, and
who ought to know—the League “seems to have no defi-

THE LEADER.

MarcH 4. 1905,

nite policy as to the disposal and direction of its orga-
ni.serg," :? would mppea.rl to an outsider that a Central
Executive may not be of its nature quite the class of body
to dispose of and direct the prga.niscrs, and a satisfactory
way out of the difficulty {Illght probably be reached by
placing the disposal and direction as well as the financial
responsibility of as many organisers as possible on en-
tities composed of a county or collection of counties.
During the eleven months ending January Z?Isb. a sum of
£1,245 s, was spent directly on organisers salaries and
expenses without taking into account any part of the
head office expenses that may have been incurred by the
attempt to dispose of and direct all this organising
energy. It is a pity that the very highest possible,
practical results should not be obt{uned from the ser-
vices of these organisers, and according to Father Bren-
nan, a member of the Executive, “ at present the League
seems to have no definite policy as to the disposal and
direction of its organisers.”

Wo hope the demonstration against the Treasury in
relation to the payment of that important body of the
Civil Service, the “ National Teachers,” may have some
effect. Dr. Hyde, in the course of his speech, publicly
requested Mr. John Redmond to ask a certain question
in the British House of Commons. This request must
have rubbed the Green Hungarian Band men the wrong
way, because, as we understand, these champions of tho
King's Most Excellent Majesty, the Lords and Commons
of Ireland, they are of opinion that no Irishmen should
go to the Imperial Parliament. ~ When Mr. Redmond
vose to speak, we read in the I'reeman that, in addition
to enthusiastic cheers, there was some hissing from a sec-
tion of the audience. Whether the hissing came from
the Green Hungarian Band men, from men who still re-
main steadfast to the cult of the Tin Pike, or from the
anti-Irishmen induced to the meeting by the Gaelic
League advertisement in the Dust Bin, or from all three
parties combined, our readers can guess for themselves;
for our own part, we suspect that the first two parties
divided the hissing honours between them, for we do not
suppose that the Gaelic League advertisement in the
anti-Irish Dust Bin induced any anti-Irish followers of
that organ to attend the meeting. Mr. John Redmond
concluded his speech in this way:—Let me, in conclu-
sion, say, that what I can do, and what my colleagues
can do as a Parliamentary Party, is at the cisposal of the

EORGE is a name from over the water. Streets
are called after him in our Isle of Saints. Bren-
dan is a national asset. We do not name streets after
him, for have we not partially forgotten his existence.
He has, however, bestowed his name on a mountain which
stands facing a waste of waters, grim, towering, cternal.
Brendan Mountain will last; George street will dwindle
and disappear—a mason’s labourer will, one day, come
with pick and shovel, hurl its mouldering bricks and
crumbling: mortar to the ground in a dusty heap, and
cart it away to make room for a line of better dwellings
which may, perchance, be re-christened Brendan street.
Assuredly it will be so; it must be so, for we are travel-
ling thitherward, whomsoever it may displease.

There are men called George in Kerry, good men, T
trow. Are there men called Brendan there? Doubtful.
Have we a Senan, a Finnchua, or a Colman called of
Latinists Columbanus? The LEADER, with customary in-
sight, has, on more than one occasion, drawn attention
to the loss of national dignity evinced in encumbering
helpless babes with foreign names. The nation, however,
is struggling to shake off its senility, as is made manifest
such names as Brendan, Ciaran, Columcille, to name
but a few of the young hopefuls who, to the writer’s
owledge, are strutting forth into boyhood, and will
nd-bye ask:—“ Why am T so called?” The answer
11 be forthcoming, for we are endeavouring, amongst
ther labours, to weave a native ecclesiastical literature
th patience and toil out of the scattered remnants of
the past.

An effort in that direction is before me as T wite; a
hoolk of 100 pages excellently brought out by the Society
for the Preservation of the Irish Language. The book is
named “ Saother ar Sean i gCein,” which may be inter-
preted into the language of the English—* The Labours
of Our Old (Trish) Abroad.” It is a notable book, indica-

Its great merit is compression and vividness of

B ALTIMORE is a village on a headland jutting out
into the Atlantic from the Southern coast of Co.
Cork. Cut away from the mainland by a narrow sound
are Have, Skerkin, and Clear Island. Before the “bad
times,"” as the famine years are commonly called by the
people, the inhabitants of Baltimore and its neighbour-
hood managed, by fishing along the coast, to pay heavy
rents for the use of plots of poor land on which they
grew patches of potatoes and corn. For a remote placa
they had a considerable fishing fleet. But “black ’47”
brought the blight to more than their staple food. It
wiped out the young and the strong, the bread-winners
of the place. The inhabitants of the islands off the
coast crowded into Skibbereen, in hopes of saving them-
selves from starvation and death. Some got temporary
employment at road-making under the Board of Works,
but many died of hunger or of the famine-fever, and
were buried in a common grave which can to-day be
identified by a grass-grown mound. It serves as a
memorial of their martyrdom, for they were the victims
of duty disowned by those in power till it was too late
to save them. Of those who survived, the emigrant
ship took away many who used to man their little crafts
along the coast. A visitor to the place thus describes
what he saw there in 1863 : — “When I first visited Cape
Clear, a couple of hookers and small rowing boats were
all that remained of what had been a respectable fishing
fleet. Beside there were only to be seen a few wrecks
lying on the strand at North Harbour, with the green
sea-weed climbing over their mouldering timber. Ves-
sels, nets, gear—all went in the agony of craving for
food ab any price, and in the period succeeding there
were no means to replace them, and it seemed even as
if the energy to desire their replacement was wanting,”*
‘Tather Leader, who was then parish priest of the dis-
trict, enlisted the sympathy of the Baroness .Burdet-tw
Coutts in favour of the people. Besides giving tem-
porary relief she helped a number of them across to
Canada, and gave them the means of following their
avocation of fishermen. It is strange that they had to
cross thie Atlantic to catch fish whilst they had them in
shoals almost ab their feet along their native coast.
Yeb it had to be so, and their benefactress did what

seemed best to her under the circumstances.

The threatenings of famine appeared again in 1879.
Those in power were warned in time of what was com-
ing, bub they heeded not the warning. Father Leader
had died some years before, and Father Davis, who was

- now parish priest of Baltimore saw in sadness the doom

of his people. His forecast of the impending ruin was
all the more painful, because the famine could so easily
be fought and kept away from them by the

~ means of living, even of prosperity, which daily came up
~ to their very doors if they only had the power to use

England is an empire whereon the hunger never sets.

Grat.—While we are all here now, let us for the

bigot will be compelled to make his living out of other
bigots. And whereas a Scotchman who depends upon
another Scotchman for his living, must either emigrate
or starve, so a bigot who looks for meat to another
bigot would soon either yield up the ghost from hunger,
or run mad out of the country.
